does: This paper introduces Concept-SAE, which extends Sparse Autoencoders by adding a structured interface for user-defined concepts. The framework decomposes model activations into two orthogonal components: Concept Tokens that are explicitly aligned to semantically meaningful concepts through dual supervision (concept existence and spatial localization), and Free Tokens that capture remaining information like standard SAEs. The method ensures Concept Tokens are faithful, spatially grounded, and cleanly separated from residual features while maintaining SAEs' capacity for open-ended discovery. The authors demonstrate through experiments that Concept-SAE produces high-fidelity, well-localized, and strongly disentangled concept representations.
connects: This work builds on the sparse autoencoder literature that has emerged as a tool for mechanistic interpretability and feature discovery in neural networks. It responds to limitations of passive SAE approaches by incorporating ideas from concept-based interpretability methods that seek to align model representations with human-understandable semantics. The dual supervision approach draws on multi-task learning principles, combining concept classification with spatial localization similar to techniques used in vision and grounding problems. The orthogonal decomposition strategy reflects broader work on disentangled representations and subspace analysis in neural networks, extending SAEs beyond unsupervised feature discovery toward controlled concept probing.
enables: Concept-SAE opens avenues for more systematic interpretability research by allowing practitioners to rigorously test whether models encode specific concepts and where those concepts localize in activations. This controllable interface enables better evaluation of model behavior with respect to user-specified semantics, supporting applications in auditing, safety analysis, and debugging. The disentanglement guarantees could facilitate interventional work where concepts are manipulated to understand causal effects on model outputs. The framework also enables hybrid approaches combining automatic feature discovery with human knowledge, potentially accelerating the discovery of policy-relevant features in large models and supporting development of more transparent and controllable AI systems.
